ontology.foodex2_description: Biscuits and cookies — sweet baked goods made from flour, sugar, fat and eggs
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for commercial cookies; SNOMED CT coverage for prepared baked snack foods is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 3 cookies weighing approximately 120 g, as offered at sandwich and salad establishments such as Subway Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Cookies, chocolate chip, commercially prepared (SR Legacy ID 18158)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input per_100g values (energy 500 kcal, protein 4.76 g, fat 23.81 g, carbohydrate 66.67 g, sugar 42.86 g, sodium 238.1 mg, dietary fibre 2.38 g, saturated fat 14.29 g, trans fat 0.48 g, cholesterol 71.43 mg, calcium 23.81 mg, iron 1.43 mg) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for commercially prepared chocolate chip cookies (energy ~488 kcal/100 g, protein ~5.4 g, fat ~23.5 g, carbohydrate ~65 g). Input values are retained as they align closely with the USDA reference and are plausible for a Subway-style commercial cookie. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients not reported in input are set to -1 where USDA data is not directly mappable to this specific product formulation.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 59 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for commercially prepared cookies (shortbread/butter cookie type, GI ~55–65, median 59). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (59 × 66.67 g/100g × 120 g serving / 100) = (59 × 80.0 g) / 100 = 47.1. This is a high glycemic load per serving, consistent with the large carbohydrate content of a 3-cookie serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (42.86 g/100g), high saturated fat (14.29 g/100g), and moderate sodium (238 mg/100g) with minimal dietary fibre (2.38 g/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit commercial cookies due to very high sugar content (42.86 g/100g) and high saturated fat (14.29 g/100g), both of which promote arterial stiffness and endothelial dysfunction; if consumed, limit to one small cookie on rare occasions and pair with a high-fibre, low-sodium meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high sugar (42.86 g/100g) and high refined carbohydrate (66.67 g/100g) with low dietary fibre (2.38 g/100g) producing a high glycemic load of 47.1 per 3-cookie serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid commercial cookies as the combination of 42.86 g/100g sugar, 66.67 g/100g refined carbohydrate, and only 2.38 g/100g dietary fibre produces a glycemic load of 47.1 per 3-cookie serving, causing a severe and rapid postprandial blood glucose spike that is incompatible with glycaemic management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (14.29 g/100g), trans fat (0.48 g/100g), high sugar (42.86 g/100g), and cholesterol (71.43 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid commercial cookies due to high saturated fat (14.29 g/100g) and trans fat (0.48 g/100g), which raise LDL cholesterol and promote atherosclerosis; the high sugar content (42.86 g/100g) further contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia and systemic inflammation, making this food unsuitable for regular consumption in a heart-healthy diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat (14.29 g/100g), trans fat (0.48 g/100g), and very high sugar (42.86 g/100g) are strongly pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid commercial cookies as saturated fat (14.29 g/100g) far exceeds the 5 g/100g threshold, trans fat (0.48 g/100g) is strongly pro-inflammatory at any amount, and sugar (42.86 g/100g) promotes inflammatory cytokine production, all of which may exacerbate joint inflammation and disease activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cookies (3 Pieces) refers to a serving of three classic Western-style baked cookies, as commonly sold at sandwich and salad chain establishments such as Subway Singapore. Each cookie is a soft-baked, sweet biscuit made from wheat flour, sugar, butter, eggs, and flavouring agents such as chocolate chips, oatmeal and raisins, or macadamia nuts. The texture is characteristically soft and chewy in the centre with slightly crisp edges, and the flavour is rich, buttery, and sweet. A standard 3-piece serving weighs approximately 120 g. Nutritionally, these cookies are energy-dense at 500 kcal per 100 g, with a very high sugar content of 42.86 g/100g, high refined carbohydrate at 66.67 g/100g, and high saturated fat at 14.29 g/100g. Trans fat is present at 0.48 g/100g. Dietary fibre is low at 2.38 g/100g. The glycemic index is approximately 59 (medium) but the glycemic load per 3-cookie serving is very high at 47.1, making this food clinically unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The food is vegetarian but not vegan due to the presence of butter and eggs. It is not gluten-free. In Singapore, these cookies are widely available as a dessert or snack add-on at fast-casual Western dining outlets and are popular across all age groups.
common_user_queries: How many calories in 3 Subway cookies?
Is a cookie healthy for diabetics?
Are Subway cookies high in fat?
Can I eat cookies if I have high blood pressure?
Calories in chocolate chip cookies per 100g
meal_context: side item at fast-casual Western restaurant

confidence_reason: Input per_100g values from GrabFood are consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for commercially prepared chocolate chip cookies and are retained. Confidence is medium rather than high because the exact Subway Singapore cookie formulation is not available in an approved primary nutrient database; values are adapted from the closest USDA SR Legacy match. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for commercially prepared cookies.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific commercial cookie product is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 18158 or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. potassium_mg set to -1: not reported in the input record and not directly mappable from the USDA reference for this specific product. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific commercial cookie product not available in the approved sources consulted. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for commercially prepared cookies; cookies contain no vitamin C. Two portions are noted in the input: 3 pieces (120 g, default) and 6 pieces (252 g); the 3-piece serving is used as the canonical default. The 6-piece portion has a slightly higher per-piece weight (42 g vs 40 g per cookie) which may reflect a different product size or weighing variance. Both is_default flags were set to true in the input, which is an inconsistency; only the 3-piece serving is treated as default here. Halal status is not confirmed for Subway Singapore cookies as ingredient sourcing (e.g., emulsifiers, flavourings) is not verified against an approved halal certification body.

improvement_actions_taken: Mapped input per_100g values to RESULT template field names (e.g., energy → energy_kcal, fat → fat_g).
Added mandatory Singaporean multilingual names: Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi.
Added comprehensive aliases_by_language and flat aliases array with full consistency check.
Sourced nutrient data from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (adapted match) replacing forbidden GrabFood source.
Added GI of 59 (medium) from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 as analogous estimate.
Calculated glycemic_load_per_serving as 47.1 for 120 g serving.
Added FoodOn and FoodEx2 ontology anchors; SNOMED CT set to null with gap note.
Replaced input health_suitability with health_context covering Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes (key type2_diabetes), and Cardiovascular Disease with clinical guidance.
Set -1 for unavailable micronutrients and documented all -1 fields in notes.
Set vitamin_c_mg to 0.0 with confirmed zero justification in notes.
Added full LLM training section with natural_language_description exceeding 200 characters.
Corrected dual is_default=true inconsistency in portions; 3-piece serving used as canonical default.
